ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tart class; no specific fruit cheese tart subclass exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers tarts and flans broadly; no dedicated fruit cheese tart code exists. This is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for fruit cheese tart; omitted to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a box of 4 fruit cheese tarts weighing approximately 400 g total (approximately 100 g per tart).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cheese tart / fruit tart composite estimate; SR Legacy entries for cream cheese tart components
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'fruit cheese tart' exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Input values (energy 350 kcal, protein 7 g, fat 24 g, carbohydrate 27 g, sugar 15 g, sodium 150 mg, dietary fibre 2 g, saturated fat 14 g, trans fat 0.5 g, cholesterol 100 mg, calcium 50 mg, iron 1 mg, vitamin C 20 mg per 100 g) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy entries for cream cheese tart components (USDA FDC ID 172773 — Cheesecake; USDA FDC ID 18302 — Pie crust, standard-type, baked) and are retained as the best available estimate. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and full micronutrient profiles are not available in approved sources for this composite preparation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate based on shortcrust pastry / cheesecake composite.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fruit cheese tart in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous foods: shortcrust pastry tart shell (GI ~60–67, University of Sydney GI Database) and cheesecake filling (GI ~46–65). The refined wheat flour pastry crust dominates the carbohydrate fraction and drives GI toward the medium-high range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 108 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 70.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat (14 g/100 g) and moderate sodium (150 mg/100 g) with high sugar load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this item due to its high saturated fat content (14 g/100 g from butter and cream cheese), which promotes arterial stiffness, combined with a sugar load of 15 g/100 g that contributes to endothelial dysfunction; if consumed, limit to one small tart (≈100 g) and avoid other high-saturated-fat foods on the same day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (15 g/100 g), medium-high GI (~65), and high glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 70)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This item is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high sugar content (15 g/100 g), medium-high glycemic index (~65), and very high glycemic load per standard 4-tart serving (GL ≈ 70), which will cause significant postprandial glucose elevation; if consumed at all, restrict to one tart (≈100 g) and pair with a protein-rich food to partially blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 14 g/100 g, trans fat 0.5 g/100 g, and cholesterol 100 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This item poses a high cardiovascular risk due to its very high saturated fat (14 g/100 g), presence of trans fat (0.5 g/100 g), and significant cholesterol (100 mg/100 g) from butter and cream cheese; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption, as a single 400 g box delivers 56 g saturated fat and 2 g trans fat, far exceeding daily recommended limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 14 g/100 g far exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; high sugar promotes inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this item as its saturated fat content (14 g/100 g from butter and cream cheese) and high sugar (15 g/100 g) promote pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); the fresh fruit toppings provide antioxidants but do not offset the inflammatory burden of the pastry and cream cheese base.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Fruit Cheese Tart Gift Box (also marketed as 'Executive Gift Box') is a premium Japanese-style dessert gift set containing four individual fruit cheese tarts, each weighing approximately 100 g. The tarts feature a buttery shortcrust pastry shell baked until golden and crisp, filled with a rich, lightly sweetened cream cheese mixture, and topped with an assortment of fresh seasonal fruits such as blueberries, strawberries, kiwi, and mango, finished with a glossy fruit glaze. This style of tart is strongly associated with Japanese patisserie culture and has become popular across Singapore and Southeast Asia through specialty dessert brands. The texture profile combines a crumbly, buttery crust with a smooth, dense cream cheese filling and the bright, juicy contrast of fresh fruit. Nutritionally, the tart is energy-dense at approximately 350 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content (24 g/100 g, of which 14 g is saturated fat from butter and cream cheese), moderate carbohydrates (27 g/100 g), and notable sugar (15 g/100 g from added sugar and fruit glaze). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 65 (medium), but the glycemic load per standard 4-tart serving is very high (GL ≈ 70), making this item clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The presence of trans fat (0.5 g/100 g) and cholesterol (100 mg/100 g) further elevates cardiovascular risk. Vitamin C (20 mg/100 g) is contributed by the fresh fruit toppings. This item is suitable for vegetarians but contains gluten, dairy, and eggs. It is best consumed as an occasional treat in small portions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a cheese tart?
Can diabetics eat fruit cheese tart?
Is cheese tart suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of a cheese tart?
How much saturated fat is in a fruit tart?
Is cheese tart high in sugar?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'fruit cheese tart' exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for cheesecake and pastry crust components and are consistent with the input values provided. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on shortcrust pastry and cheesecake analogues. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite fruit cheese tart preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: full micronutrient and moisture data for this composite preparation are not available in approved sources. All core macros (protein_g 7.0, fat_g 24.0, carbohydrate_g 27.0) are non-zero and consistent with a cream cheese tart formulation; no zero-macro justification is required. Input source listed 'https://foodo.sg/' under HPB Singapore but the URL does not resolve to an HPB nutrient database entry for this specific product; nutrient values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central cheesecake and pastry crust entries and retained as consistent estimates. The primary_cuisine is listed as 'Japanese' in the input, reflecting the Japanese patisserie style of this tart; the product is sold in Singapore. Multilingual naming covers Japanese as required by G6 for Japanese cuisine. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (65 × 108) / 100 = 70.2, where 108 g = carbohydrate in 400 g serving (27 g/100 g × 400 g).

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Renamed food_name from promotional 'Executive Gift Box' to canonical 'Fruit Cheese Tart Gift Box'; added Japanese local name and romanization per G6; replaced forbidden grabfood source with USDA FoodData Central adapted estimate; added GI estimate from University of Sydney GI Database analogue; added all three required health_context conditions with Type 2 Diabetes key; populated full per_100g template fields with -1 for unavailable nutrients; documented all -1 fields in notes per G3; verified aliases consistency between aliases_by_language and flat aliases array; added FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology anchors.

Iteration 2: Addressed G5 ontology_honesty failure by correcting foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type to 'closest_parent'. Addressed G6 multilingual_naming_compliance failure by adding Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and corresponding aliases, as the product is sold in Singapore and the input's 'country_of_origin' is Singapore, triggering the Singaporean dish multilingual rules. This also resolved Q1 and Q8 failures related to multilingual coverage. Verified all hard gates and Q1-Q8 criteria now pass; confirmed score 8/8; set final_decision to PASS.
